title: Tool
name: NetScan
desc: a small c program that scans your network for devices and open ports. it also displays hostname(if found) vendor and more.

aboutParagraphs: NetScan is a command-line network scanner built from scratch in C. It auto-detects your local\n subnet, ping-sweeps it to find live hosts, and identifies each device through DNS, mDNS, NetBIOS, and SSDP lookups, falling back to a MAC vendor lookup when nothing else resolves. Open ports are found with a multi-threaded TCP connect-scan, with lightweight banner grabbing for identifying running services. Results can be exported to JSON or CSV, and a diff mode flags new or missing devices between scans. I built it to get hands-on with raw sockets, threading, and Linux networking internals in C — there's still plenty I'd like to add, but it's a solid, working tool as it stands.

title: Pi Project
name: Raspberry Pi Wi-Fi to Ethernet Router
desc: Configured a Raspberry Pi 3B as a Wi-Fi-to-Ethernet router using NetworkManager, dnsmasq, and nftables.

aboutParagraphs:
Overview
--------
This project turns a Raspberry Pi 3B into a functional router that bridges a Wi-Fi internet connection to a wired Ethernet network. Devices plugged into the Pi -- directly or through a switch -- receive DHCP-assigned IPs and route their traffic out through the Pi's Wi-Fi interface, with no dependency on the host network's own router.

Internet
       |
     Wi-Fi
       |
  Raspberry Pi 3B
       |
   Ethernet
       |
  Switch / Laptop

Stack
-----
- Raspberry Pi OS Lite (32-bit) -- minimal base OS, no desktop overhead
- NetworkManager (nmcli) -- interface configuration, replacing the legacy dhcpcd approach used in older Pi OS releases
- dnsmasq -- DHCP server for the Ethernet-side clients
- nftables -- NAT/masquerade rules and firewalling
- sysctl -- kernel-level IP forwarding
- Tailscale -- pre-existing VPN mesh on the Pi, which also touches nftables and had to be accounted for rather than conflict with

Network Design
---------------
- Pi's Ethernet interface (eth0): static IP 10.10.10.1/24, acting as gateway
- DHCP range for clients: 10.10.10.100 - 10.10.10.200
- DNS handed out to clients: 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8
- NAT: eth0 traffic masqueraded out through wlan0

1. IP forwarding wasn't where expected
   /etc/sysctl.conf was empty because current Pi OS expects drop-in files under /etc/sysctl.d/. Fixed by adding net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 in a new 99-ipforward.conf and applying with sysctl --system.

2. No dhcpcd.service
   Bookworm replaced dhcpcd with NetworkManager entirely, so the old restart-the-daemon workflow didn't apply. All interface config went through nmcli connection modify instead.

3. Static IP on eth0
   Configured directly via NetworkManager rather than editing interface files by hand, then verified with ip addr.

4. dnsmasq failed on startup
   Error was "unknown interface eth0," because it was starting before the interface existed and was bound too strictly. Switching bind-interfaces to bind-dynamic let dnsmasq wait for the interface instead of failing immediately.

5. Client didn't reconnect after a cable pull
   NetworkManager didn't re-activate the profile automatically. Fixed per-incident with nmcli connection up, and permanently with connection.autoconnect yes.

6. No dhclient on the client laptop
   Modern NetworkManager-based distros don't ship the ISC DHCP client by default -- this is expected, not a bug, so the fix was just using nmcli connection down/up instead of reaching for dhclient.

7. Tailscale nftables warnings
   Tailscale manages its own NAT table via iptables-nft and logs a warning about it. This is benign -- confirmed the custom masquerade rule for wlan0 was still intact alongside it.

Verification
------------
Interfaces and routes were checked with ip addr / ip route, forwarding confirmed via /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward, dnsmasq status via systemctl status dnsmasq, active NAT rules via nft list ruleset, and issued leases via /var/lib/misc/dnsmasq.leases.

Result
------
The laptop received an IP from the Pi, used it as its default gateway, and retained full internet access with its own Wi-Fi disabled -- confirming the Pi was successfully routing all traffic.
